text: The development of a large abdominal hernia is an extremely rare complication after ventricular peritoneal shunt placement. Here we present a case of a large symptomatic ventral hernia in a morbidly obese woman with a BMI of fifty eight.

Four trocars are placed in the left lateral quadrant, including two for robotic arms, a 12mm camera trocar, and one assistant port. The surgeon begins dissection using a monopolar hook to remove part of the hernia sac, leaving a small portion intact and transecting it at a high point.

Four trocars are positioned in the left lateral quadrant: two for robotic arms, a 12mm camera trocar, and one assistant port. The surgeon begins dissection using a monopolar hook to remove part of the hernia sac, leaving a small portion intact and transecting it at a high point.

The surgeon bends the bipolar instrument to achieve effective retraction without grasping tissue. The monopolar hook is used in a bottom-up fashion for posterior dissection, allowing simultaneous dissection and retraction. After dissecting the hernia sac, a second larger cavity containing bowel loops is revealed.

The surgeon bends the bipolar instrument to achieve effective retraction without grasping tissue. Using a hook in a bottom-up fashion, the posterior dissection is performed, revealing a second larger cavity containing bowel loops. Round tip eight millimeter scissors are used for careful dissection near the small bowel, with the left hand gently opening the plane to avoid injury to the bowel.

The surgeon uses round tip eight millimeter scissors for careful dissection near the small bowel, avoiding unnecessary energy spread to the surrounding serosa. The left hand gently opens the plane, minimizing risk of injury to the small bowel. The video shows a complete fusion between the small bowel and peritoneum, a common occurrence in patients with ventricular peritoneal shunts.

The surgeon uses a robotic platform to navigate challenging exposure planes, rotating a thirty-degree camera ninety degrees to visualize the fusion between the small bowel and peritoneum, which is common in patients with ventricular peritoneal shunts.
The surgeon utilizes a robotic platform to rotate a thirty-degree camera ninety degrees, enhancing exposure of challenging planes within the surgical field.

The surgeon completes the dissection, allowing the catheter to pass through the hernia defect. Small deserosalizations are repaired using interrupted stitches of PDS-IV-O, with no deeper injuries observed upon inspection.

The surgeon completes the dissection, allowing the catheter to pass through the hernia defect. Small deserosalizations are repaired using interrupted stitches of PDS-IV-O. The edges of the defect are approximated with a short running suture using size one V-lock barbed sutures, which are effective for closing hernia defects under tension.
The surgeon approximates the two edges of the hernia defect using a short running suture with a size one V-lock barbed suture, effectively closing the defect under tension.

A 20x25 cm ventral light mesh with a longitudinal opening is prepared for the catheter. Temporary stay sutures are placed at the four quadrants of the mesh using straight needles to facilitate its placement, ensuring an open passage for the catheter.

The surgeon prepares a 20x25 cm ventral light mesh with a longitudinal opening for the catheter. Temporary stay sutures are placed at the four quadrants of the mesh using straight needles to facilitate its placement, ensuring an open passage for the catheter to prevent obstruction.
The surgeon creates a small opening around the catheter to prevent obstruction, then uses Proline-II-O sutures to close the mesh around the catheter, ensuring secure placement.

The surgeon creates a small opening around the catheter to prevent obstruction and uses Proline-II-O sutures to close the mesh around the catheter. Due to difficulty in placing a stay suture in one corner, the mesh is fixed using a parachute technique.
The surgeon employs a parachute technique to secure the mesh in one corner, using short 2-0 Prolene running sutures to close the longitudinal opening around the catheter.

The surgeon places short, two-oh Proline running sutures around the mesh to close the longitudinal opening, including the lateral quadrant. The technique involves rotating the fingers on the control by one hundred eighty degrees to achieve complete needle rotation.
The surgeon sutures the lateral quadrant of the mesh using a technique that involves rotating the fingers 180 degrees on the needle control to achieve complete needle rotation.
The operative time was two hours and fifty-five minutes with an estimated blood loss of less than fifty cc's.
